clean_story:
Physical form. Tangible existence.

The word object comes from Latin obiectum—meaning "thrown before" or "presented to the senses." It’s anything you can touch, see, or measure: a rock, a chair, a star.

Objects resist change. A steel beam bends only under extreme force; a diamond’s carbon atoms lock so tightly it takes 1,200 degrees Celsius to melt it. Even fragile things—like a soap bubble—hold their shape until air pressure or a fingertip disrupts their surface tension.

Some objects defy expectations. A boomerang’s curved wings generate lift like an airplane, but its spin flips the airflow, pulling it back. A mirror isn’t just glass; its silver coating scatters light in precise angles, returning your reflection without absorbing colors.

Humans rely on objects to extend ability. A lever turns 50 pounds of force into 500. A telescope’s curved lenses bend light from galaxies 13 billion years old, making the invisible visible.

Three related subjects are material resistance, tool evolution, and how light interacts.
